text: Successful endurance athletes typically distribute training intensity so that about 80% of sessions are performed at low intensity (below 2 mM blood lactate) and about 20% involve high-intensity work.
High-intensity interval training in a polarized model should target approximately 90% of VO2max.
Nationally and internationally competitive endurance athletes typically train 10 to 13 sessions per week.

Increasing the proportion of high-intensity interval training beyond the typical 80/20 distribution does not provide convincing evidence of long-term performance gains in already well-trained athletes.
The combination of predominantly low-intensity, long-duration training with fewer high-intensity bouts may optimize adaptive signaling while maintaining an acceptable level of physiological stress.
Low-intensity training should be performed below the 2 mM blood lactate threshold to qualify as the easy portion of polarized training distribution.
